paper_title: \LARGE \bf Variational Neural Belief Parameterizations for Robust Dexterous Grasping under Multimodal Uncertainty

perturbations.why_wrong: The author list associates Clinton Enwerem with affiliation 1. Changing the superscript to 3 makes it inconsistent with the thanks note which lists Enwerem as being with affiliation 1 (Department of Electrical & Computer Engineering).

The text defines the grasp wrench space (GWS) as the convex hull of resistible hand wrenches. In set theory and robotics, the complement notation (c) would imply the space of wrenches the hand cannot resist, which contradicts the definition of the GWS as the set of resistible wrenches.

The text explicitly refers to the 'terminal' inscribed-ball radius. The subscript 'T' typically denotes terminal time or state in the provided context of sequential decision processes, whereas '0' denotes the initial state, creating a contradiction with the word 'terminal'.

The perturbation changes the Robot Arm degrees of freedom (DoF) to 12 in the TikZ code, which contradicts the figure caption and the 'Problem Formulation' section which both explicitly state the FR3 cobot has 6 DoF.
The text immediately preceding this span states that 'time is indexed discretely from t=0'. Changing the sequence to start at t=1 creates an internal inconsistency within the same paragraph.
The text immediately following the span defines the action as a '6-dimensional vector of finger joint velocity commands', making a 3-dimensional vector space inconsistent with the description.

The context specifies that at each discrete time $t$, the system applies an action $\mathbf{a}_t$ and receives a corresponding observation. Using $t+1$ for the observation at time $t$ breaks the causal indexing of the discrete-time system described.

The text defines $\mathbf{w}$ as object-frame contact wrenches. In 3D robotic grasping, a wrench consists of 3 force components and 3 torque components, totaling 6 dimensions, as stated in the original text.

The caption describes the neural belief dynamics updating the latent embedding at the 'current decision step'. Updating a past embedding $h_{t-1}$ using current actions and observations contradicts the forward-filtering nature of the framework.

The object pose $\boldsymbol{\xi}_{o,t}$ must be an element of the Special Euclidean group $SE(3)$ to represent rigid body transformation in 3D space; stating it is not an element contradicts the fundamental definition of a pose estimate.

The definition of the belief state at time t as a posterior distribution should include the current observation o_t. Changing the index to o_{1:t-1} makes the belief a prior (prediction) rather than the posterior distribution defined in the text.
The text states that the observation likelihood is factored into terms. In probability theory, independent or conditionally independent factors in a joint likelihood must be multiplied, not added.

The objective function for a trajectory optimization problem should evaluate the cost at each time step t based on the state at that time step theta_t. Using the initial state theta_0 for all terms in the summation ignores the system dynamics.

The location-scale reparameterization trick for a Gaussian distribution N(mu, sigma^2) is defined as mu + sigma * epsilon. Changing the sign to subtraction is mathematically inconsistent with the standard reparameterization used for gradient estimation in variational inference.
The Gumbel-Softmax trick requires samples from a standard Gumbel distribution, which is Gumbel(0,1). Changing the location parameter to 1 introduces a bias in the selection weights zeta_k.

The dual form of CVaR requires adding the expected excess over the VaR threshold (eta) to eta itself; changing the plus to a minus would result in a value that decreases as the tail risk increases, contradicting the definition of a risk measure.

The CVaR definition at confidence level beta requires scaling the tail expectation by 1/(1-beta). Replacing this with 1/beta is inconsistent with the standard dual form of CVaR cited in the context (Rockafellar 2000).

The left-hand side specifies the gradient with respect to the action vector 'a', but the right-hand side is perturbed to take the gradient of the cost function with respect to the latent parameters 'theta', which is mathematically incorrect for an action-optimization step.

In the friction-limited robustness cost C_rho, the term involving the friction coefficient mu_i should be subtracted because higher friction (larger mu) should reduce the cost (increase robustness). Changing the sign to plus makes higher friction increase the cost.

The context states that lambda_c blends the expected cost and CVaR such that lambda_c=0 yields the expected cost and lambda_c=1 yields CVaR. The term (1+lambda_c) fails this logic, as lambda_c=1 would result in 2*E[C_t] instead of 0.
